metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_2564
metadata.comment:
Rationale: From Results section:

"DN-14-3-3η showed reduced binding to the RIM1α fragment (22% ± 2%, mean ± s.e.m., of the wild-type 14-3-3η; Fig. 2a,b), indicating that this mutant could be used to test the importance of 14-3-3's interaction with RIM1a in cerebellar LTP induction.

"The combined treatment of brain lysate with a cAMP analog, Sp-cAMP-S, and a phosphatase inhibitor, okadaic acid (treatments that have been shown to increase Ser413 phosphorylation in cerebellar synaptosomes5), produced a pronounced increase in RIM1α binding to GST-14-3-3η."

"When granule cells were transfected with wild-type 14-3-3η or an empty vector, tetanic stimulation (4 Hz × 100 pulses) caused a marked potentiation that lasted for the duration of the recording (188% ± 11% and 207% ± 11% of baseline at t = 25 min, respectively; n = 7 per group), which was consistent with what has previously been reported for untreated cell pairs3,5. Transfections with DN-14-3-3η, however, produced a near total inhibition of LTP (111% ± 13% at t = 25 min; n = 8) while leaving post-tetanic potentiation (at t = 2.5 min) mostly intact (Fig. 3a)."

"Sp-8CPT-cAMP-S produced a slowly developing potentiation when presynaptic granule cells were transfected with either an empty vector or wild-type 14-3-3η (224% ± 22% and 211% ± 23% of baseline, respectively, measured at t = 25 min; n = 6 per group). However, transfection with DN-14-3-3η produced strong inhibition of this chemically induced potentiation (127% ± 11% of baseline, n = 7), as did RIM1α-V415P (122% ± 10% of baseline, n = 7)."

22/2/2018 Pim
- Fig.1: YWHAH binds the presynaptic protein RIM1alpha. Experimental description: From Results section:
"Voltage-clamp recordings from granule cell-Purkinje cell pairs in cultures of embryonic mouse cerebellum5 were made together with presynaptic transfection with 14-3-3η"

22/2/2018 Pim
- Species of overexpressed YWHAH is not stated. Orgin traced back to PMID:9710607 authors were contacted for information (dd.22/2/2018, ashaw@wustl.edu). Answer:

"I"m pretty sure, but not 100% sure that this is mouse."
